frameworks.name: GABAergic inhibition and perceptual learning
frameworks.one_line: task-dependent cortical inhibition mediates learning-related plasticity in sensory cortex

frameworks.name: noise suppression vs feature template retuning
frameworks.one_line: dissociable inhibitory mechanisms support distinct learning subprocesses

frameworks.name: BOLD fMRI signal
frameworks.one_line: BOLD alone cannot disentangle inhibitory from excitatory contributions to plasticity

frameworks.name: tDCS cortical excitability modulation
frameworks.one_line: perturbing excitability during training causally tests inhibition's role in learning

core_question: Does GABAergic inhibition in sensory cortex causally support distinct mechanisms of perceptual learning?
key_assumptions: MR spectroscopy GABA reflects task-relevant inhibitory tone
BOLD signal alone cannot dissociate inhibition from excitation
perceptual learning involves separable noise-filtering and template-tuning processes
manipulations: training task type (noise filtering vs feature template retuning)
tDCS perturbation of cortical excitability during training
combined fMRI and MR spectroscopy measurement
key_findings: GABAergic inhibition in occipito-temporal cortex relates to functional plasticity and behavioral improvement
increased GABA accompanies feature template retuning
tDCS perturbation alters performance in a task-specific manner
interpretations: task-dependent GABAergic inhibition implements dissociable learning mechanisms
cortical suppression is directly linked to behavioral improvement in perceptual learning
